diagrams.title: Classification of Java Control Statements
diagrams.description: A hierarchical tree diagram showing the three main categories of control statements in Java
elements: Root node: 'Java Control Statements' with three main branches
Branch 1: Selection Statements (if, if-else, if-else-if ladder, switch)
Branch 2: Iteration Statements (for, while, do-while, enhanced for)
Branch 3: Jump Statements (break, continue, return)
Each branch connects to its constituent statements with downward arrows
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the complete taxonomy of control statements and can categorize any control statement they encounter. This provides a mental framework for understanding when to use each type.

diagrams.title: If-Else-If Ladder Execution Flow
diagrams.description: A vertical flowchart showing the decision-making process through an if-else-if chain
elements: Start diamond shape at top
First decision: 'Condition 1?' with Yes/No paths
If Yes: Execute 'Block 1' then arrow to 'End'
If No: Arrow to second decision 'Condition 2?'
Continue similarly for Condition 2, 3
Final 'else' connects to 'Default Block' then to 'End'
All paths converge at single 'End' oval
Arrows show linear top-to-bottom flow with branching
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes how the if-else-if ladder evaluates conditions sequentially and executes only the first matching block. Helps students understand short-circuit evaluation and the importance of condition order.

diagrams.title: Loop Structures Comparison Flowchart
diagrams.description: Side-by-side flowcharts comparing for, while, and do-while loops
elements: Three columns, each with a loop flowchart
FOR LOOP: Initialization box → Condition diamond → Body box → Update box → back to Condition
WHILE LOOP: Condition diamond → Body box → back to Condition (initialization outside)
DO-WHILE LOOP: Body box → Condition diamond → back to Body (guaranteed at least one execution)
Highlight the key difference: do-while has body before condition
Dotted box around do-while showing 'Execute at least once' property
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the structural differences between the three loop types and clarifies when each is appropriate. The visual comparison helps students choose the right loop for different scenarios.

diagrams.title: Break vs Continue vs Return Flow
diagrams.description: A comparative flowchart showing control flow for jump statements within loops
elements: Main loop structure shown as rounded rectangle
BREAK: Arrow exiting the loop box entirely with label 'Exits loop completely'
CONTINUE: Arrow jumping to loop's update/condition with label 'Skips current iteration, proceeds to next'
RETURN: Arrow pointing upward out of method with label 'Exits method, returns value if specified'
Color coding: Break (red), Continue (yellow), Return (green)
diagrams.purpose: Clarifies the often-confused behavior of jump statements. Students can visualize exactly where control transfers when each statement is executed, preventing common programming errors.

diagrams.title: Switch Statement Execution Flow
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing how switch-case evaluates and selects matching cases
elements: Start → Expression evaluation box (value in parentheses)
Diamond decision with multiple case branches
Each case shows: 'Case value N?' → Yes → Execute 'Block N' → Break statement → Exit switch
Default case shown as final diamond with 'No match?'
Arrow from default to 'Execute Default Block' then to Exit
Highlight: Break statement prevents fall-through
Arrow showing fall-through path without break (dashed line)
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates switch statement mechanics including case matching, break statements, default case, and the fall-through behavior. Helps students understand why breaks are necessary and when fall-through is intentional.

charts.title: Selection Statements Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table with rows for if, if-else, if-else-if, and switch. Columns: Statement Type, Use Case, Condition Type, Number of Paths, Typical Application. For if: single decision, boolean condition, 1 path. For if-else: binary decision, 2 paths. For if-else-if: multiple conditions, multiple paths. For switch: multi-way based on value matching, multiple cases plus default.

charts.title: Iteration Statements Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table with rows for for, while, do-while, and enhanced for. Columns: Syntax Pattern, When to Use, Initialization, Condition Check, Guarantee First Execution. for: counter-controlled, inside parentheses. while: condition-controlled, pre-test. do-while: condition-controlled, post-test guarantees execution. enhanced for: collection/array iteration, no index management.

charts.title: Choosing a Control Statement Decision Tree
charts.content: Decision flowchart: Start → 'Need to make a decision?' (Yes/No). If No → 'Need to repeat?' (Yes/No). If No → 'End (sequential)'. If Yes for decision → 'One condition or multiple?' → Single → 'Use if' or 'Use if-else'. Multiple → 'Compare values or boolean conditions?' → Values → 'Use switch'. Boolean → 'Use if-else-if'. If Yes for repeat → 'Know iteration count?' (Yes/No). Yes → 'Use for loop'. No → 'Need at least one execution?' → Yes → 'Use do-while'. No → 'Use while loop'.

charts.title: Jump Statements Quick Reference
charts.content: Summary table with columns: Statement, Purpose, Applicable To, Behavior. Rows: break - exits loop/switch, applicable to loops and switch, transfers control to end of structure. continue - skips iteration, applicable to loops only, jumps to next iteration. return - exits method, applicable to methods, returns value if specified. throw - exception handling, applicable to methods/blocks, transfers control to exception handler.

mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview linking all control statements under three main categories. The mind map helps students recall the complete set of control statements and understand their grouped purpose. Useful for exam revision and quick reference.

branches: - Executes block if condition is true
mindMaps.purpose: Focuses specifically on selection/decision-making statements, helping students understand when to use each type and the differences between boolean conditions versus value-based comparisons. Important for writing conditional logic in JSP and servlets.
